_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: MD/MS/ DNB/ PG Diploma or equivalent (in Ophthalmology)
clinical_indications: Unilateral cataract (to be operated as early as possible)
Bilateral cataract (to be operated as early as possible)
Symmetric cataract (second eye is operated within 1 week of the first eye/ same sitting)

preauth.label: Clinical notes (detailing Indication for Lensectomy /pediatric lens aspiration/ membranectomy and supporting investigation)

claim.label: Detailed Discharge summary with visual outcomes
Intraoperative photograph with time and date (optional)

ppd_preauth.q: Detailed Clinical notes with history, etiology, clinical symptoms, examination and investigation details (visual acuity, direct ophthalmoscopy, ultrasound of eye, etc.)?
Clinical photo of Affected part with Proper labelling of Affected Eye whether R or L with full face photograph.

cpd_claim.q: Do OT notes detail about General anesthesia, steps of surgery performed and outcomes of the surgery?
Are the documents available to show appropriate post-op care, advise including for follow-up advise?
Was the intra operative photograph submitted?
Is the barcode/ sticker of IOL available, if used?

common_queries: History, clinical signs and symptoms, or examination findings do not suggest the presence of pediatric cataract.
Clinical notes are incomplete or do not detail indications and supporting investigations for the procedure.
Clinical photograph of affected eye is missing or lacks proper R/L labelling or full face view.
Operative notes do not detail general anesthesia, steps of surgery, or outcomes.
Barcode or sticker of the Intraocular Lens (IOL) is missing, if an IOL was used.

clinical_key_pointers: Proceed for Surgery for Pediatric Cataract only if diagnosis made is backed by clinical signs, symptoms, ophthalmic examination and does not respond to conservative medical therapy.

Definition: Pediatric cataract is a treatable leading cause of childhood blindness. Untreated cataracts in children lead to tremendous social, economic, and emotional burden to the child, family, and society. It accounts for 7.4%–15.3% of pediatric blindness. The prevalence is more in low income groups than high income ones. It has been found that during pregnancy, 67% of the mothers of such children had a history of illness and 22% had taken medications during pregnancy. It is usually diagnosed during routine screening/ patient has leukocoria/ strabismus.
Etiology: Most cataracts are idiopathic in nature.
Etiology: Hereditary
Etiology: Metabolic cataract
Etiology: Congenital infections (Toxoplasma, rubella, cytomegalovirus, herpes, and syphilis infections- TORCH)
Etiology: Traumatic cataract
Etiology: Associated with other systemic conditions such as juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Leukocoria (lens opacity/ clouding)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Not able to make eye contact
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Squint
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Microphthalmos (small eye)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Buphthalmos (large eye)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Nystagmus (abnormal movement of the eye)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): Difficulty in viewing distant objects (such as a blackboard)
Signs & Symptoms (one or more of the following): May also be associated with features of other systemic illnesses such as those of Down's syndrome
Examination & Investigations: Visual acuity assessment
Examination & Investigations: Direct ophthalmoscopy
Examination & Investigations: Screening of parent's eye esp. of mother
Examination & Investigations: Ultrasound biomicroscopy of the eye
Examination & Investigations: A scan
Examination & Investigations: Keratometry
Examination & Investigations: MRI/ Color doppler (if there is suspicion of Persistent fetal vasculature-PFV)
Examination & Investigations: X-ray / CT scan (in cases with h/o trauma)
Examination & Investigations: TORCH profile (Toxoplasma, rubella, cytomegalovirus, herpes, and syphilis infection) (in case of bilateral congenital cataract)
Examination & Investigations: Evaluation by Rheumatologist/ Neurologist/ Nephrologist (based on presence of features of systemic illness, if any)
Complications: Amblyopia (lazy eye)
Complications: Glaucoma
Complications: Retinal detachment
Complications: Postoperative inflammation
Complications: Infection of eye
additional_information.post_op_care: Prescription of glasses; Amblyopia therapy; Suture removal and repeat refraction at 1 month post-op & at 3 months post-op
